title: Félix Verdejo

infobox.value: Félix Giomar Verdejo Sánchez  (1993-05-19) May 19, 1993 (age 32)  [San Juan](/wiki/San_Juan,_Puerto_Rico "San Juan, Puerto Rico"), Puerto Rico
* [killing an unborn child](/wiki/Foeticide "Foeticide") * [kidnapping resulting in death](/wiki/Kidnapping "Kidnapping")
Keishla Rodríguez Ortiz, 27 and her unborn child
[Laguna San José](/wiki/Laguna_San_Jos%C3%A9_(Puerto_Rico) "Laguna San José (Puerto Rico)"), San Juan

(born May 19, 1993) is a Puerto Rican former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") and convicted murderer. As an [amateur](/wiki/Amateur_boxing "Amateur boxing") he represented Puerto Rico at the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/2012_Olympics "2012 Olympics"). Between 2012 and 2020 Verdejo competed professionally for [Top Rank](/wiki/Top_Rank "Top Rank") and held the [WBO](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") Latino [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") title from 2015 to 2017.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Puerto Rican boxer and convicted murderer (born 1993)

In this [Spanish name](/wiki/Spanish_name "Spanish name"), the first or paternal [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") is  *Verdejo* and the second or maternal family name is  *Sánchez*.

Félix Verdejo

**Félix Giomar Verdejo Sánchez** (born May 19, 1993) is a Puerto Rican former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") and convicted murderer. As an [amateur](/wiki/Amateur_boxing "Amateur boxing") he represented Puerto Rico at the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/2012_Olympics "2012 Olympics"). Between 2012 and 2020 Verdejo competed professionally for [Top Rank](/wiki/Top_Rank "Top Rank") and held the [WBO](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") Latino [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") title from 2015 to 2017.

In May 2021, Verdejo turned himself in to police to face federal charges in the [murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder") of his pregnant [mistress](/wiki/Mistress_(lover) "Mistress (lover)"). In 2023, he was found guilty and sentenced to life in prison.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Amateur career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Amateur career")]

Verdejo was the winner at lightweight at the [2012 American Boxing Olympic Qualification Tournament](/wiki/2012_American_Boxing_Olympic_Qualification_Tournament "2012 American Boxing Olympic Qualification Tournament").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Qualification was assured when Verdejo defeated a [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") rival in the quarter-finals since the top-4 finishers qualified for the 2012 Olympics in his [weight class](/wiki/Weight_class_(boxing)#Amateur_boxing "Weight class (boxing)").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

At the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_lightweight "Boxing at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Men's lightweight"), Verdejo defeated [Panamanian](/wiki/Panama "Panama") José Huertas in the first round with a score of 11–5.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Then he defeated [Tunisian](/wiki/Tunisian_people "Tunisian people") [Ahmed Mejri](/wiki/Ahmed_Mejri "Ahmed Mejri") with a 16–7 score before losing to [Vasyl Lomachenko](/wiki/Vasyl_Lomachenko "Vasyl Lomachenko") of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") in the quarterfinals 14–9.

Verdejo was also a Panamerican Youth Champion.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Professional career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo_vs._Petchsamuthr_Duanaaymukdahan.jpg/250px-F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo_vs._Petchsamuthr_Duanaaymukdahan.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo_vs._Petchsamuthr_Duanaaymukdahan.jpg)

Verdejo vs. Petchsamuthr Duanaaymukdahan at [CotaiArena](/wiki/CotaiArena "CotaiArena") in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") in November 2013

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

In October 2012, a 19 year old Verdejo became a professional by joining [Top Rank](/wiki/Top_Rank "Top Rank"), signing for an undisclosed amount that reportedly included a bonification $600,000.

Verdejo won the "New Hispanic Promise" category of the [2013 Premios Juventud](/wiki/2013_Premios_Juventud "2013 Premios Juventud") on July 18 of that year. After closing his 2013 campaign, Verdejo was voted "Prospect of the Year" by BoxingScene and Top Rank.[[6]](#cite_note-6) He also finished second in Yahoo!'s list, which noted that any other year he would have been a "slam dunk winner".[[7]](#cite_note-7) In his first fight of 2014, Verdejo faced undefeated Lauro Alcantar, winning by knockout in only 21 seconds. Verdejo continued his success throughout 2014, going 7–0 with 6 KO, making his career record 16–0 (KO 12). [ESPN.com](/wiki/ESPN.com "ESPN.com") would go on to name Verdejo 'Prospect of the Year' for 2014.

#### WBO Latino champion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: WBO Latino champion")]

Verdejo would get his first chance to claim his first title, it was announced in January 2015. Top Rank announced Mexican Marco Antonio López (24–5, 15 KOs) as his opponent in a scheduled 8 round bout for February 28, however this was later changed to a 10-round bout and took place on April 25, due to Verdejo injuring his hand.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The fight was contested for the vacant [WBO Latino](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") title, which was only at stake for Verdejo as Lopez weight 4 pounds over the lightweight limit.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The fight took place in Puerto Rico as Verdejo claimed the vacant title via a fifth-round technical knockout. At the time of stoppage, all three judges had it 40–36 in favour of Verdejo.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Verdejo made his first defence against undefeated American Ivan Najera (16–0, 8 KOs) on June 13 at the [Madison Square Garden Theater](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). Verdejo, making his [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") debut, defeated Najera in a wide unanimous decision to retain the title. Verdejo scored two knockdowns and outpointed Najera with two judges scoring the bout 100–88 and one judge had it 99–89. Verdejo connected with 40% of his punches thrown compared to 30% from Najera.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

On December 11, Verdejo made a second successive defence of his WBO Latino title in Puerto Rico against Brazilian Josenilson Dos Santos (27–3, 17 KOs). In round two, Verdejo hit a big right hand that dropped dos Santos, although dos Santos beat the count, referee Roberto Ramirez Jr. stopped the fight after a count was given.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Verdejo started off 2016 with a defence against undefeated Brazilian William Silva (23–0, 14 KOs) on the undercard of [Crawford](/wiki/Terence_Crawford "Terence Crawford")-[Lundy](/wiki/Hank_Lundy "Hank Lundy") on February 27, 2016, at the Madison Square Garden Theater.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The fight was televised on HBO. Verdejo landed 106 of 421 total punches as he cruised to a one sided 10 round unanimous decision win against Silva who connected 45 of 325 total punches. Two judges had it 100–90 for Verdejo and the third judge had it 99–91.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Verdejo would next fight two months later, returning to Puerto Rico against 29 year old Mexican Jose Luis Rodriguez (18–8, 11 KOs), who was on a four fight win streak, on April 16 in a 10-round bout at the [Coliseo Roberto Clemente](/wiki/Roberto_Clemente_Coliseum "Roberto Clemente Coliseum"). Verdejo was originally supposed to fight [Daniel Evangelista](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Evangelista,_Jr.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Daniel Evangelista, Jr. (page does not exist)") (18–6–2, 14 KOs), who withdrew from the fight due to [tendonitis](/wiki/Tendinitis "Tendinitis").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Verdejo won a one sided 10 round unanimous decision win over Rodriguez. He won the fight by the judges scoring it 99–91, 99–91 and 98–92. Verdejo used his power and speed advantage to punish Rodriguez with big shots to the head and body throughout the ten rounds. With this win, Verdejo was guaranteed a return to HBO at the Madison Square Garden Theater on June 11 against lightweight contender [Jose Felix Jr.](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_F%C3%A9lix,_Jr. "José Félix, Jr.") (33–1–1, 25 KOs).[[18]](#cite_note-18) Following this win, [WBA](/wiki/World_Boxing_Association "World Boxing Association") ranked Verdejo at #2 lightweight and [WBO](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") ranked him #3.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)

Verdejo fought on the undercard of the title fight between [WBO super featherweight](/wiki/List_of_super-featherweight_boxing_champions "List of super-featherweight boxing champions") champion [Martinez](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1n_Mart%C3%ADnez_(boxer) "Román Martínez (boxer)") vs. [Lomachenko](/wiki/Vasyl_Lomachenko "Vasyl Lomachenko") at [Madison Square Garden Theater](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden_Theater "Madison Square Garden Theater") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") on June 11, 2016. He defended his Latino title against 2nd tier Mexican boxer Juan Jose Martinez (25–2, 17 KOs), who had won six fights in a row since a seventh-round technical decision loss to [Rey Bautista](/wiki/Reynaldo_Bautista "Reynaldo Bautista") in September 2014. Verdejo was supposed to fight Felix Jr. however a deal could not be made, according to [Top Rank](/wiki/Top_Rank "Top Rank") vice president Carl Moretti, Felix asked for about 'four times the money available' for Verdejo's opponent.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Verdejo, who had been criticized for his previous two decision wins, stopped Martinez in five rounds to retain his title. Verdejo was the aggressor, out-landing Martinez 90 to 36 and connected on 51 percent of his power shots. In Round 5, Martinez had a cut above his right eye and with 20 seconds remaining, Verdejo pinned Martinez against the ropes, before referee Michael Ortega jumped in at 2:40 and stopped the fight. Verdejo told his translator after the bout that he 'wants to fight the best'. Bob Arum spoke of the possibility of a world title fight between Verdejo and WBO champion [Terry Flanagan](/wiki/Terry_Flanagan_(boxer) "Terry Flanagan (boxer)") for early November, however this would no longer be the case due to Verdejo suffering injuries in a motorcycle accident.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

On December 6, 2016, Verdejo announced that he was close to returning to the ring, slated to be for February 3, 2017, in his native Puerto Rico.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Weeks later Top Rank confirmed that he would indeed fight on that date against former super featherweight title challenger Oliver Flores (27–2–2, 17 KOs) at the [Coliseo Roberto Clemente](/wiki/Roberto_Clemente_Coliseum "Roberto Clemente Coliseum") in San Juan. Flores is coming off a stoppage loss in December 2015 to then WBA junior lightweight champion [Takashi Uchiyama](/wiki/Takashi_Uchiyama "Takashi Uchiyama"). The fight will aired live on UniMas.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Verdejo won the fight via a 10-round unanimous decision. The three judges scored the fight 99–91, 98–92, 96–94 all in favor or Verdejo. With the win Verdejo landed a shot at the WBO lightweight title.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### World title mix

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World title mix")]

On June 12, 2017, the WBO president [Francisco Valcarcel](/wiki/Francisco_Varcarcel "Francisco Varcarcel") received word that promoters Bob Arum and Frank Warren were working closely together to come to an agreement for a fight to take place between Verdejo and undefeated WBO lightweight champion [Terry Flanagan](/wiki/Terry_Flanagan_(boxer) "Terry Flanagan (boxer)"). Some sources claimed the fight would take place on September 9 in Manchester.[[29]](#cite_note-29) There was a hold up in the negotiations due to Verdejo being offered $175,000, however his representatives were looking for him to earn in the region of $450,000 having to travel to Flanagan's home country to challenge for the title.[[30]](#cite_note-30) A deal was finally reached for the fight to take place on September 16, 2017, at the [Copper Box Arena](/wiki/Copper_Box_Arena "Copper Box Arena") in London. WBO middleweight champion [Billy Joe Saunders](/wiki/Billy_Joe_Saunders "Billy Joe Saunders") was confirmed to take part on the same card. The fight would also mark the fight time Flanagan would fight in London.[[31]](#cite_note-31) On July 17, Warren emailed Top Rank to make them aware that Flanagan had suffered a leg injury, thus postponing the fight.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The WBO requested that medical evidence had to be sent to them. Flanagan's team confirmed the evidence would be sent the next day.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

On August 5, 2017, Top Rank revealed that Verdejo would stay busy and fight on the September 22 undercard of [Gilberto Ramírez](/wiki/Gilberto_Ram%C3%ADrez "Gilberto Ramírez") vs. [Jesse Hart](/wiki/Jesse_Hart "Jesse Hart") in [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona").[[34]](#cite_note-34) On August 14, Top Rank announced that Verdejo would fight Mexican boxer [Antonio Lozada Jr.](/wiki/Antonio_Lozada_Jr. "Antonio Lozada Jr.") (37–2, 31 KOs) at the [Convention Center](/wiki/Tucson_Convention_Center "Tucson Convention Center") in a 10 round bout.[[35]](#cite_note-35) It would have marked the first time Lozada Jr. would ever fight professionally outside of Mexico, however Verdejo picked up an injury in training, forcing him to pull out of the fight.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In December 2017, WBO president Francisco Valcarcel stated that Verdejo would need to work his way back into the mandatory position. With Flanagan moving to light welterweight and vacating his WBO lightweight title, the WBO ordered their #1 [Ray Beltrán](/wiki/Ray_Beltr%C3%A1n "Ray Beltrán") to fight #3 [Paulus Moses](/wiki/Paulus_Moses "Paulus Moses") for the vacant title.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Career from 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Career from 2018")]

On December 22, 2017, it was announced that Verdejo would return to the ring after 13 months' inactivity at the Madison Square Garden Theater on March 17, 2018.[[38]](#cite_note-38) A week later, [Antonio Lozada Jr.](/wiki/Antonio_Lozada_Jr. "Antonio Lozada Jr.") was lined up to fight Verdejo. Lozada was scheduled to fight Verdejo in February 2017. In January 2018, Lozada signed the deal to make the fight official.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Verdejo suffered his first loss as a professional, being stopped in round 10. Despite the ring rust, having not fought in over a year, going into the final round Verdejo was ahead 87-84 and 86-85 on two scorecards with Lozada ahead 86-85 on the remaining scorecard. In round 10, Lozada knocked Verdejo down once. After Verdejo beat the count and got to his feet, Lozada began to unload more shots which led to referee Eddie Claudio stopping the fight at 2:37 of the round.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Following his first professional career defeat, Verdejo took eight months out and returned to the ring in Puerto Rico on November 10, 2018, against Mexican journeyman Yardley Armenta Cruz. The fight took place at the [Mario Quijote Morales Coliseum](/wiki/Mario_Morales_Coliseum "Mario Morales Coliseum") in Guaynabo. Verdejo returned to winning ways after stopping Cruz in round 2. It was a left hook which sent him down and he was unable to recover.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

On April 20, 2019, Verdejo fought his second fight after his first loss, on the Crawford vs Khan undercard at the Madison Square Garden, against Bryan Vasquez, ranked #7 by the WBA at lightweight.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Verdejo won by a 10-round unanimous decision, with the scorecards reading 97–93, 97–93, 98–92. It was a good win, but fans and media alike thought the once world-class prospect left a lot to be desired.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

After that fight, some critics pointed out that Verdejo could use a new trainer in the future. In October 2019, he was announced to have met with numerous trainers in the United States, who were interested in working with him.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The following month, he announced that he will be working with renowned boxing trainer Ismael Salas, known for training Cuban greats like [Guillermo Rigondeaux](/wiki/Guillermo_Rigondeaux "Guillermo Rigondeaux"), [Luis Ortiz](/wiki/Luis_Ortiz_(Cuban_boxer) "Luis Ortiz (Cuban boxer)") and [Erislandy Lara](/wiki/Erislandy_Lara "Erislandy Lara"), in a bid to regain control and restart his career.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In his first fight with a new trainer, Felix battled Manuel Rey Rojas to a unanimous ten-round decision. The first few rounds proved to be tougher for the Puerto Rican, but as the fight went on go got up to speed and managed to secure a clear win. Despite securing a clear win, critics were not impressed by the win, mostly because Verdejo felt like a blue-chip prospect and a star-in-making after the 2012 Olympics, but still hasn't delivered on the same level in the professional game.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

On December 12, 2020, Verdejo fought for the vacant [WBO](/wiki/WBO "WBO") Inter-Continental [Lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") title against [Masayoshi Nakatani](/wiki/Masayoshi_Nakatani "Masayoshi Nakatani"). Verdejo became the first boxer to knock down Nakatani, once in the first round and again in the fourth round. However Nakatani knocked Verdejo down twice in the ninth and the fight was stopped 1:45 into the round.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Verdejo was born in [San Juan, Puerto Rico](/wiki/San_Juan,_Puerto_Rico "San Juan, Puerto Rico"), and has two brothers and two sisters. Verdejo, the only boxer in the family, started boxing when he was nine years old.

On August 7, 2016, Verdejo was involved in a motorcycle accident while traveling from [Fajardo](/wiki/Fajardo,_Puerto_Rico "Fajardo, Puerto Rico") to [Carolina, Puerto Rico](/wiki/Carolina,_Puerto_Rico "Carolina, Puerto Rico"). At first listed in critical condition, he recuperated quickly at a local hospital. He was with a companion, who was uninjured. He suffered a head injury and small lacerations.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Verdejo was released from the hospital on August 19.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Murder charges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_Verdejo&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Murder charges")]

On April 30, 2021, Verdejo was named by police as a [person of interest](/wiki/Person_of_interest "Person of interest") in the disappearance of a 27-year-old pregnant woman, Keishla Rodríguez. Rodriguez's family said that she was pregnant with Verdejo's child.[[52]](#cite_note-Bartolomei_2021-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Her body was found on the [San José Lagoon](/wiki/Laguna_San_Jos%C3%A9_(Puerto_Rico) "Laguna San José (Puerto Rico)") the next day.[[55]](#cite_note-55) On May 2, Verdejo turned himself in to police and appeared in court via video link, pleading not guilty on May 11. It was revealed on January 31, 2022, that Verdejo would not be facing the death penalty.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)
On July 28, 2023, and after more than 25 hours of deliberation, a jury found Verdejo guilty in the [kidnapping](/wiki/Kidnapping "Kidnapping") and killing of his pregnant mistress, as well as the killing of an unborn child. The jury could not reach [unanimous verdicts](/wiki/Hung_jury "Hung jury") on the other two charges against Verdejo, which included [carjacking](/wiki/Carjacking "Carjacking") resulting in the death of a person, as well as possession and use of a firearm while committing a violent act. On November 3, 2023, Verdejo was sentenced to life in prison.[[59]](#cite_note-59)
